{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.7,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.4",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "R1.7",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "When you say “Make this stronger,” do you want A) primarily sentence-level polish for clarity and punch, B) stronger positioning and novelty claims (tempering or substantiating “first” claims with citations and aligning claims to evidence), C) a restructured abstract/narrative arc, or D) all of the above (if so, please give a priority order among A/B/C)?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Strengthening likely involves sharpening the thesis, gap, and contribution framing above the sentence level.",
    "R1.2": "Ensuring the argument from method and results to claims like ‘deployment-ready’ is logically consistent will make it more compelling.",
    "R1.3": "Claims such as 92% of GPT-4 at 18% cost and ‘first demonstration’ must precisely match the evidence presented.",
    "R1.4": "The text makes ‘first’ claims that require careful positioning versus nearest prior work to credibly assert novelty.",
    "R1.5": "Reordering the abstract’s elements (problem, method, results, implications) may produce a stronger narrative arc.",
    "R1.6": "Sentence- and paragraph-level edits (clarity, concision, tone) are essential to make the passage read as stronger.",
    "R1.7": "Strengthening ‘first’ and framing claims typically requires adding or adjusting citations and attributions to related work.",
    "RX.1": "We should explicitly disclose uncertainty around ‘first’ and generalization beyond the reported tasks.",
    "RX.2": "We must avoid overclaiming (e.g., ‘deployment-ready,’ ‘reference architecture’) unless tightly supported.",
    "RX.3": "Edits should remain within the scope of strengthening the provided passage without inventing new results.",
    "RX.4": "We should avoid creating hype or misleading implications that could prompt misuse or over-reliance on unsupported claims.",
    "RX.5": "Any strengthened claims should be traceable to specific results, datasets, and the described judging/training setup."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: When you say “Make this stronger,” do you want A) primarily sentence-level polish for clarity and punch, B) stronger positioning and novelty claims (tempering or substantiating “first” claims with citations and aligning claims to evidence), C) a restructured abstract/narrative arc, or D) all of the above (if so, please give a priority order among A/B/C)?

rationale.R1.1: Strengthening likely involves sharpening the thesis, gap, and contribution framing above the sentence level.
rationale.R1.2: Ensuring the argument from method and results to claims like ‘deployment-ready’ is logically consistent will make it more compelling.
rationale.R1.3: Claims such as 92% of GPT-4 at 18% cost and ‘first demonstration’ must precisely match the evidence presented.
rationale.R1.4: The text makes ‘first’ claims that require careful positioning versus nearest prior work to credibly assert novelty.
rationale.R1.5: Reordering the abstract’s elements (problem, method, results, implications) may produce a stronger narrative arc.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence- and paragraph-level edits (clarity, concision, tone) are essential to make the passage read as stronger.
rationale.R1.7: Strengthening ‘first’ and framing claims typically requires adding or adjusting citations and attributions to related work.
rationale.RX.1: We should explicitly disclose uncertainty around ‘first’ and generalization beyond the reported tasks.
rationale.RX.2: We must avoid overclaiming (e.g., ‘deployment-ready,’ ‘reference architecture’) unless tightly supported.
rationale.RX.3: Edits should remain within the scope of strengthening the provided passage without inventing new results.
rationale.RX.4: We should avoid creating hype or misleading implications that could prompt misuse or over-reliance on unsupported claims.
rationale.RX.5: Any strengthened claims should be traceable to specific results, datasets, and the described judging/training setup.
